national_expectations.summary: After back-to-back group-stage exits at the two previous World Cups ended a long run of reaching at least the quarter-finals, the football culture treats deep progress as close to a birthright, so the bar is to break that cycle and at minimum reach the latter stages; previews frame the quarter-finals as the realistic expectation with a semi-final reachable if the draw opens up and the creative players fire, while a full title run is seen as a stretch given inconsistency in the biggest moments.

sources.outlet: Wikipedia / Germany at the FIFA World Cup

national_mood.summary: Mood soured sharply after the final-group defeat to Ecuador, with German coverage describing the squad as disillusioned even though they had already won the group; the loss ended a strong run and raised real doubts about defensive solidity and attacking sharpness heading into the knockouts, mixing relief at progressing with anxiety that the team has not yet looked ready for knockout football.

manager_situation.summary: Julian Nagelsmann was appointed to reverse the recent slide and arrived having reached the Euro 2024 quarter-finals on home soil; reporting notes his contract runs to twenty-twenty-eight but carries a break clause that would have let the federation part ways in the event of a group-stage exit, so qualifying from the group eases the most immediate pressure, but he came across as irritable after the Ecuador defeat, sharply rejecting suggestions his players lacked commitment and facing questions over heavy rotation and his selection calls.

historical_context.summary: Germany are four-time world champions, with titles in nineteen-fifty-four, nineteen-seventy-four, nineteen-ninety and twenty-fourteen, and they are among the most consistent deep finishers in tournament history, second only to Brazil for reaching at least the quarter-finals; but the team crashed out at the group stage at both of the previous two World Cups, in Russia and in Qatar, ending a long run of consecutive tournaments in which they had always reached at least the quarter-finals and denting a footballing identity built on routinely going deep.

sources.outlet: Wikipedia / Germany at the FIFA World Cup

dominant_storylines.storyline: A jolting final-group defeat to Ecuador despite Germany having already topped Group E, which ended their unbeaten run and exposed defensive fragility and loose possession; Nagelsmann said there was 'too much freestyle' after his side took an early lead, and German coverage framed it as a wake-up call rather than a harmless dead rubber.

Selection and rotation debates around Nagelsmann: questions over the impact of crowd favourite Deniz Undav off the bench, over the goalkeeping picture around veteran captain Manuel Neuer, and over his heavy changes against Ecuador, with the coach insisting he rotated deliberately and trusts every player in the squad.

The creative young duo of Jamal Musiala and Florian Wirtz carries Germany's hopes: Musiala returned to fitness after a serious broken-leg and dislocated-ankle injury suffered last summer and has been backed by Nagelsmann as one of the outstanding players in world football even short of full sharpness, with the pair seen as dovetailing well in the final third; both were quiet in the Ecuador loss, intensifying scrutiny on whether they fire in the knockouts.

Confirmed defensive blow: centre-back Nico Schlotterbeck has been ruled out for the rest of the tournament with an ankle injury, thinning Germany's defensive options just as the press questions their backline solidity going into knockout football.

attributed_quotes.quote_original: Nein, bitte hört auf mit dem Quatsch, ehrlich
attributed_quotes.context: Snapping back at a question suggesting his side lacked engagement, after the 2-1 group-stage defeat to Ecuador. German original from t-online.
Reflecting on the squad's mood after the final-group defeat to Ecuador despite already having topped the group.

local_availability_reporting.status_reported: Missed the Ecuador match with an adductor problem but is reported as expected to be fit for the Round of 32 against Paraguay.

gaps: R32 opponent now confirmed by multiple major outlets as Paraguay (a best-placed third team), Match 74 at Gillette Stadium, Foxborough, on Jun 29; next_opponent_code set to PAR. Note the supplied DB still listed the opponent as best-third/TBD, so reconcile the DB before relying on it downstream.

Venue naming: outlets variously call the R32 venue the 'Boston Stadium' and Gillette Stadium in Foxborough — these are the same venue (Greater Boston); the World-Cup-branded 'Boston Stadium' is Gillette Stadium.

No official FIFA / DFB (German federation) primary-source statement was found for national mood, expectations, or the manager's standing — all such claims rest on press/discovery sources, not official tier.

Could not independently verify from a second outlet a specific 'expects to reach the quarter-finals' bar as a federation or fan-consensus expectation; the quarter-final framing comes primarily from a single preview's prediction plus historical context.
No verbatim quote could be sourced from Musiala or Wirtz themselves about their own form ahead of the R32; their storyline is built on third-party reporting and Nagelsmann's comments.

Olympics.com (a Musiala source in the draft) repeatedly timed out and could not be read; the injury-and-return narrative is corroborated instead via Bundesliga.com plus ESPN/FOX/NBC reporting on the broken leg and recovery.

source_data_snapshot.outlet: Wikipedia / Germany at the FIFA World Cup
